html:
XVI

Next morning when Brent went down to breakfast he was as a man who had passed through an illness. His eyes were bloodshot, his face was pale, his step was nervous and weak.

вАЬJust what I expected,вАЭ muttered Mrs.¬†Jones. вАЬHe was in a beastly condition last night. I shall speak to Mr.¬†Perkins about it. He had no right to take and get him in such a state.вАЭ

She was more incensed than ever when the gay young clerk came in looking perfectly fresh. вАЬHeвАЩs used to it,вАЭ she told herself, вАЬand it doesnвАЩt tell on him, but itвАЩs nearly killed that poor young man.вАЭ

вАЬHullo there, Brent,вАЭ said Perkins. вАЬYou chucked me for good last night. Did you lose your way, or was your вАШcharacterвАЩ too interesting?вАЭ

вАЬCharacter too interesting,вАЭ was the laconic reply.

вАЬAnd IвАЩll bet youвАЩve been awake all night studying it out.вАЭ

вАЬYou are entirely right there,вАЭ said Brent, smiling bitterly. вАЬI havenвАЩt slept a wink all night: IвАЩve been studying out that character.вАЭ

вАЬI thought you looked like it. You ought to take some rest today.вАЭ

вАЬI canвАЩt. IвАЩve got to put in my time on the same subject.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Jones pursed her lips and bustled among the teacups. The idea of their laughing over their escapades right before her face and thinking that she did not understand! She made the mental observation that all men were natural born liars, and most guilty when they appeared to be most innocent. вАЬCharacter,вАЭ indeed! Did they think to blind her to the true situation of things? Oh, astute woman!

вАЬStrange fellow,вАЭ said Perkins to his spoon, when, after a slight breakfast, Brent had left the table.

вАЬThereвАЩs others that are just as strange, only they think theyвАЩre sharper,вАЭ quoth Mrs.¬†Jones, with a knowing look.

вАЬI donвАЩt understand you,вАЭ returned her boarder, turning his attention from his spoon to the ladyвАЩs face.

вАЬThereвАЩs none so blind as those who donвАЩt want to see.вАЭ

вАЬAgain I say, I donвАЩt understand you, Mrs.¬†Jones.вАЭ

вАЬOh, Mr.¬†Perkins, itвАЩs no use trying to fool me. I know men. In my younger days I was married to a man.вАЭ

вАЬStrange contingency! But still it casts no light on your previous remarks.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩve got very innocent eyes, I must say, Mr.¬†Perkins.вАЭ

вАЬThe eyes, madam, are the windows of the soul,вАЭ Perkins quoted, with mock gravity.

вАЬWell, if the eyes are the soulвАЩs windows, there are some people who always keep their windows curtained.вАЭ

вАЬBut I must deny any such questionable performance on my part. I have not the shrewdness to veil my soul from the scrutiny of so keen an observer as yourself.вАЭ

вАЬOh, flattery isnвАЩt going to do your cause one mite of good, Mr.¬†Perkins. IвАЩm not going to scold, but next time you get him in such a state I wish youвАЩd bring him home yourself, and not let him come tearing in here like a madman, scaring a body half to death.вАЭ

вАЬWill you kindly explain yourself? What condition? And who is вАШhimвАЩ?вАЭ

вАЬOh, of course you donвАЩt know.вАЭ

вАЬI do not.вАЭ

вАЬDo you mean to tell me that you werenвАЩt out with Mr.¬†Brent last night before he came home?вАЭ

вАЬI assuredly was not with him after the first quarter of an hour.вАЭ

вАЬWell, itвАЩs hard to believe that he got that way by himself.вАЭ

вАЬThat way! Why, he left me at the door of MeyerвАЩs beer-garden to talk to a temperance crank who he thought was a character.вАЭ

вАЬWell, no temperance character sent him rushing and stumbling in here as he did last night. вАШCharacter,вАЩ indeed! It was at the bottom of a pail of beer or something worse.вАЭ

вАЬOh, I donвАЩt think he was вАШloaded.вАЩ HeвАЩs an author, and I guess his eye got to rolling in a fine frenzy, and he had to hurry home to keep it from rolling out of his head into the street.вАЭ

вАЬMr.¬†Perkins, this is no subject for fun. I have seen what I have seen, and it was a most disgraceful spectacle. I take your word for it that you were not with Mr.¬†Brent, but you need not try to go further and defend him.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not trying to defend him at all; itвАЩs really none of my business.вАЭ And Perkins went off to work, a little bit angry and a good deal more bewildered. вАЬI thought he was a вАШjay,вАЩвАКвАЭ he remarked.

To Brent the day was a miserable one. He did not leave his room, but spent the slow hours pacing back and forth in absorbed thought, interrupted now and then by vain attempts to read. His mind was in a state of despairing apprehension. It needed no prophetic sense to tell him what would happen. It was only a question of how long a time would elapse before he might expect to receive word from Dexter summoning him home. It all depended upon whether or not the вАЬCalifornia PilgrimвАЭ got money enough last night for exploiting his disgraceful history to finish the last stage of the journey.

What disgusted the young man so intensely was that his father, after having led the life he had, should make capital out of relating it. Would not a quiet repentance, if it were real, have been quite sufficient? He very much distrusted the sincerity of motive that made a man hold himself up as an example of reformed depravity, when the hope of gain was behind it all. The very charity which he had preached so fiercely to his congregation he could not extend to his own father. Indeed, it appeared to him (although this may have been a trick of his distorted imagination) that the вАЬPilgrimвАЭ had seemed to take a sort of pleasure in the record of his past, as though it were excellent to be bad, in order to have the pleasure of conversion. His lip involuntarily curled when he thought of conversion. He was disgusted with all men and principles. One man offends, and a whole system suffers. He felt a peculiar self-consciousness, a self-glorification in his own misery. Placing the accumulated morality of his own life against the full-grown evil of his fatherвАЩs, it angered him to think that by the intervention of a seemingly slight quantity the results were made equal.

вАЬWhat is the use of it all,вАЭ he asked himself, вАЬmy struggle, involuntary though it was, my self-abnegation, my rigidity, when what little character I have built up is overshadowed by my fatherвАЩs past? Why should I have worked so hard and long for those rewards, real or fancied, the favour of God and the respect of men, when he, after a career of outrageous dissipation, by a simple act or claim of repentance wins the DeityвАЩs smile and is received into the arms of people with gushing favour, while I am looked upon as the natural recipient of all his evil? Of course they tell us that there is more joy over the one lamb that is found than over the ninety and nine that went not astray; it puts rather a high premium on straying.вАЭ He laughed bitterly. вАЬWith what I have behind me, is it worth being decent for the sake of decency? After all, is the game worth the candle?вАЭ

He took up a little book which many times that morning he had been attempting to read. It was an edition of Matthew ArnoldвАЩs poems, and one of the stanzas was marked. It was in вАЬMycerinus.вАЭ

Oh, wherefore cheat our youth, if thus it be,
Of one short joy, one lust, one pleasant dream,
Stringing vain words of powers we cannot see,
Blind divinations of a will supreme?
Lost labour! when the circumambient gloom
But holds, if gods, gods careless of our doom!

He laid the book down with a sigh. It seemed to fit his case.

It was not until the next morning, however, that his anticipations were realised, and the telegraph messenger stopped at his door. The telegram was signed Eliphalet Hodges, and merely said, вАЬCome at once. You are needed.вАЭ

вАЬNeededвАЭ! What could they вАЬneedвАЭ of him? вАЬWantedвАЭ would have been a better wordвБ†вАФвАЬwantedвАЭ by the man who for sixteen years had forgotten that he had a son. He had already decided that he would not go, and was for the moment sorry that he had stayed where the telegram could reach him and stir his mind again into turmoil; but the struggle had already recommenced. Maybe his father was burdening his good old friends, and it was they who вАЬneededвАЭ him. Then it was his duty to go, but not for his fatherвАЩs sake. He would not even see his father. No, not that! He could not see him.

It ended by his getting his things together and taking the next train. He was going, he told himself, to the relief of his guardian and his friend, and not because his fatherвБ†вАФhis father!вБ†вАФwanted him. Did he deceive himself? Were there not, at the bottom of it all, the natural promptings of so close a relationship which not even cruelty, neglect, and degradation could wholly stifle?

He saw none of the scenes that had charmed his heart on the outward journey a few days before; for now his sight was either far ahead or entirely inward. When he reached Dexter, it was as if years had passed since he left its smoky little station. Things did not look familiar to him as he went up the old street, because he saw them with new eyes.

Mr.¬†Hodges must have been watching for him, for he opened the door before he reached it.

вАЬCome in, Freddie,вАЭ he said in a low voice, tiptoeing back to his chair. вАЬIвАЩve got great news fur you.вАЭ

вАЬYou neednвАЩt tell me what it is,вАЭ said Brent. вАЬI know that my father is here.вАЭ

Eliphalet started up. вАЬWho told you?вАЭ he said; вАЬsome blockhead, IвАЩll be bound, who didnвАЩt break it to you gently as I would вАЩaвАЩ done. ActuвАЩlly the people in this here townвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt blame the people, Uncle вАЩLiph,вАЭ said the young man, smiling in spite of himself. вАЬI found it out for myself before I arrived; and, I assure you, it wasnвАЩt gently broken to me either.вАЭ To the old manвАЩs look of bewildered amazement, Brent replied with the story of his meeting with his father.

вАЬItвАЩs the good LordвАЩs doinвАЩs,вАЭ said Eliphalet, reverently.

вАЬI donвАЩt know just whose doing it is, but it is an awful accusation to put on the Lord. IвАЩve still got enough respect for Him not to believe that.вАЭ

вАЬFreddie,вАЭ exclaimed the old man, horror-stricken, вАЬyou ainвАЩt a-gettinвАЩ irreverent, you ainвАЩt a-beginninвАЩ to doubt, air you? DonвАЩt do it. I know jest what youвАЩve had to bear all along, anвАЩ I know what youвАЩre a-bearinвАЩ now, but you ainвАЩt the only one that has their crosses. IвАЩm a-bearinвАЩ my own, anвАЩ it ainвАЩt light neither. You donвАЩt know what it is, my boy, when you feel that somethinвАЩ precious is all your own, to have a real owner come in anвАЩ snatch it away from you. While I thought yore father was dead, you seemed like my own son; but now it вАЩpears like I ainвАЩt got no kind oвАЩ right to you anвАЩ itвАЩs kind oвАЩ hard, Freddie, itвАЩs kind oвАЩ hard, after all these years. I know how a mother feels when she loses her baby, but when itвАЩs a grown son thatвАЩs lost, one that sheвАЩs jest been pilinвАЩ up love fur, itвАЩsвБ†вАФitвАЩsвБ†вАФвАЭ The old man paused, overcome by his emotions.

вАЬI am as muchвБ†вАФno, more than ever your son, Uncle вАЩLiph. No one shall ever come between us; no, not even the man I should call father.вАЭ

вАЬHe is yore father, Freddie. ItвАЩs jest like I told Hester. She was fur sendinвАЩ him along.вАЭ In spite of himself, a pang shot through BrentвАЩs heart at this. вАЬBut I said, вАШNo, no, Hester, heвАЩs FredвАЩs father anвАЩ we must take him in, fur our boyвАЩs sake.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬNot for my sake, not for my sake!вАЭ broke out the young man.

вАЬWell, then, fur our MasterвАЩs sake. We took him in. He was mighty low down. It seemed like the Lord had jest spared him to git here. HesterвАЩs with him now, anвАЩвБ†вАФanвАЩвБ†вАФkin you stand to hear it?вБ†вАФthe doctor says heвАЩs only got a little while to live.вАЭ

вАЬOh, I can stand it,вАЭ Brent replied, with unconscious irony. The devotion and the goodness of the old man had softened him as thought, struggle, and prayer had failed to do.

вАЬWill you go in now?вАЭ asked Eliphalet. вАЬHe wants to see you: he canвАЩt die in peace without.вАЭ

The breath came hard between his teeth as Brent replied, вАЬI said I wouldnвАЩt see him. I came because I thought you needed me.вАЭ

вАЬHeвАЩs yore father, Freddie, anвАЩ heвАЩs penitent. All of us pore mortals need a good deal oвАЩ furgivinвАЩ, anвАЩ it doesnвАЩt matter ef one of us needs a little more or a little less than another: it puts us all on the same level. Remember yore sermon about charity, anвАЩвБ†вАФanвАЩ jedge not. You ainвАЩt seen all oвАЩ His plan. Come on.вАЭ And, taking the young man by the hand, he led him into the room that had been his own. Hester rose as he entered, and shook hands with him, and then she and her husband silently passed out.

The sufferer lay upon the bed, his eyes closed and his face as white as the pillows on which he reclined. Disease had fattened on the hollow cheeks and wasted chest. One weak hand picked aimlessly at the coverlet, and the laboured breath caught and faltered as if already the hand of Death was at his throat.

The young man stood by the bed, trembling in every limb, his lips now as white as the ashen face before him. He was cold, but the perspiration stood in beads on his brow as he stood gazing upon the face of his father. Something like pity stirred him for a moment, but a vision of his own life came up before him, and his heart grew hard again. Here was the man who had wronged him irremediably.

Finally the dying man stirred uneasily, muttering, вАЬI dreamed that he had come.вАЭ

вАЬI am here.вАЭ BrentвАЩs voice sounded strange to him.

The eyes opened, and the sufferer gazed at him. вАЬAre youвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI am your son.вАЭ

вАЬYouвБ†вАФwhy, IвБ†вАФsaw youвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou saw me in Cincinnati at the door of a beer-garden.вАЭ He felt as if he had struck the man before him with a lash.

вАЬDidвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФgo in?вАЭ

вАЬNo: I went to your temperance meeting.вАЭ

The elder Brent did not hear the ill-concealed bitterness in his sonвАЩs voice. вАЬThank God,вАЭ he said. вАЬYou heardвБ†вАФmyвБ†вАФstory, anвАЩвБ†вАФit leaves meвБ†вАФlessвБ†вАФto tell. SomethingвБ†вАФmade me speakвБ†вАФto you thatвБ†вАФnight. Come nearer. WillвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФshake hands withвБ†вАФme?вАЭ

Fred reached over and took the clammy hand in his own.

вАЬI haveвБ†вАФhadвБ†вАФa pore life,вАЭ the now fast weakening man went on; вАЬanвАЩ I haveвБ†вАФdone wrongвБ†вАФbyвБ†вАФyou, but IвБ†вАФhaveвБ†вАФrepented. Will you forgive me?вАЭ

Something came up into BrentвАЩs heart and burned there like a flame.

вАЬYou have ruined my life,вАЭ he answered, вАЬand left me a heritage of shame and evil.вАЭ

вАЬI know itвБ†вАФGod help meвБ†вАФI know it; but wonвАЩtвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФforgive me, my son? IвБ†вАФwant toвБ†вАФcall youвБ†вАФthatвБ†вАФjust once.вАЭ He pressed his hand closer.

Could he forgive him? Could he forget all that he had suffered and would yet suffer on this manвАЩs account? Then the words and the manner of old Eliphalet came to him, and he said, in a softened voice, вАЬI forgive you, father.вАЭ He hesitated long over the name.

вАЬThank God forвБ†вАФforвБ†вАФthe nameвБ†вАФanвАЩвБ†вАФforgiveness.вАЭ He carried his sonвАЩs hand to his lips, вАЬI shanвАЩt beвБ†вАФaliveвБ†вАФlongвБ†вАФnowвБ†вАФanвАЩ myвБ†вАФdeathвБ†вАФwill setвБ†вАФpeopleвБ†вАФto talkinвАЩ. They willвБ†вАФbringвБ†вАФup theвБ†вАФpast. IвБ†вАФdonвАЩt want youвБ†вАФtoвБ†вАФstay anвАЩ haveвБ†вАФto bearвБ†вАФit. I donвАЩt want toвБ†вАФbring any more onвБ†вАФyou than I haveвБ†вАФalready. GoвБ†вАФaway, asвБ†вАФsoon as I am dead.вАЭ

вАЬI cannot leave my friends to bear my burdens.вАЭ

вАЬThey will not speakвБ†вАФof themвБ†вАФas theyвБ†вАФwill speak ofвБ†вАФyou, myвБ†вАФpoorвБ†вАФboy. YouвБ†вАФareвБ†вАФoldвБ†вАФTom BrentвАЩsвБ†вАФson. IвБ†вАФwish I could takeвБ†вАФmy nameвБ†вАФanвАЩ allвБ†вАФit meansвБ†вАФalongвБ†вАФwithвБ†вАФme. ButвБ†вАФpromiseвБ†вАФmeвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФwillвБ†вАФgo. PromiseвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI will go if you so wish it.вАЭ

вАЬThankвБ†вАФyou. AnвАЩвБ†вАФnowвБ†вАФgoodbye. IвБ†вАФcanвАЩt talkвБ†вАФanyвБ†вАФmore. I donвАЩt dareвБ†вАФto adviseвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФafterвБ†вАФallвБ†вАФyouвБ†вАФknowвБ†вАФof me; but doвБ†вАФrightвБ†вАФdo right.вАЭ

The hand relaxed and the eyelids closed. Brent thought that he was dead, and prompted by some impulse, bent down and kissed his fatherвАЩs browвБ†вАФhis father, after all. A smile flitted over the pale face, but the eyes did not open. But he did not die then. Fred called Mrs.¬†Hodges and left her with his father while he sat with Eliphalet. It was not until the next morning, when the air was full of sunlight, the song of birds, and the chime of church bells, that old Tom BrentвАЩs weary spirit passed out on its search for God. He had not spoken after his talk with his son.

There were heavy hearts about his bed, but there were no tears, no sorrow for his deathвБ†вАФonly regret for the manner of his life.

Mrs.¬†Hodges and Eliphalet agreed that the dead man had been right in wishing his son to go away, and, after doing what he could to lighten their load, he again stood on the threshold, leaving his old sad home. Mrs.¬†Hodges bade him goodbye at the door, and went back. She was too bowed to seem hard any more, or even to pretend it. But Eliphalet followed him to the gate. The two stood holding each otherвАЩs hands and gazing into each otherвАЩs eyes.

вАЬI know youвАЩre a-goinвАЩ to do right without me a-tellinвАЩ you to,вАЭ said the old man, chokingly. вАЬThatвАЩs all I want of you. Even ef you donвАЩt preach, you kin live anвАЩ work fur Him.вАЭ

вАЬI shall do all the good I can, Uncle вАЩLiph, but I shall do it in the name of poor humanity until I come nearer to Him. I am dazed and confused now, and want the truth.вАЭ

вАЬGo on, my boy; youвАЩre safe. YouвАЩve got the truth now, only you donвАЩt know it; fur theyвАЩs One that says, вАШInasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these, ye have done it unto me.вАЩвАКвАЭ

Another hearty handshake, and the young man was gone.

As Fred went down the street, someone accosted him and said, вАЬI hear yore fatherвАЩs home.вАЭ

вАЬYes, heвАЩs home,вАЭ said Fred.

Tom Brent was buried on Tuesday morning. The Rev. Mr.¬†Simpson, who, in spite of his age, had been prevailed upon to resume charge of his church, preached the sermon. He spoke feelingly of the вАЬdear departed brother, who, though late, had found acceptance with the Lord,вАЭ and he ended with a prayerвБ†вАФwhich was a shotвБ†вАФfor the вАЬdepartedвАЩs misguided son, who had rejected his MasterвАЩs call and was now wandering over the earth in rebellion and sin.вАЭ It was well that he did not see the face of Eliphalet Hodges then.

DanвАЩl Hastings nodded over the sermon. In the back part of the church, Mrs.¬†Martin and Mrs.¬†Smith whispered together and gaped at the two old mourners, and wondered where the boy was. They had вАЬheerd he was in town.вАЭ

Bill Tompkins brought Elizabeth to the funeral.
